nativeRange.habitat: Rocky shoreline zones of Lake Tanganyika; found among and between rocks at moderate depths, often in large groups

decorPreferences: numerous caves and crevices formed by stacked rocks
slate or smooth rounded rocks mimicking Tanganyikan littoral biotope
tight rock piles with interconnecting tunnels

diet.notes: Accepts a wide range of prepared and frozen foods: high-quality sinking catfish pellets, algae wafers, frozen bloodworm, frozen brine shrimp, and daphnia. Wild fish are aufwuchs scrapers and scavengers on rocky substrates, consuming algae, invertebrates, and detritus. Feed after lights-out to ensure nocturnal feeders compete successfully. Supplement with vegetable matter (blanched courgette, spinach) and vitamin-enriched frozen foods.

goodWith: Tanganyikan cichlids (shell-dwellers such as Neolamprologus multifasciatus, Lamprologus ocellatus)
other Synodontis species of similar size
avoidWith: large aggressive cichlids that can swallow or injure small catfish
very small fish or fry (opportunistic predation risk)
fish requiring soft acidic water (incompatible water chemistry)
Congo tetras or other soft-water species (conflicting parameters)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase specimen photo for Synodontis lucipinnis; shows typical adult coloration with spotted pattern.

summary: A small, active Lake Tanganyika squeaker catfish described in 2006, long misidentified in trade as S. petricola. Virtually all “petricola” sold in hobby shops are S. lucipinnis. Reaches ~9 cm vs the true S. petricola’s 14 cm. Requires hard, alkaline Tanganyikan water; best kept in groups of 3+.
careNotes:
Synodontis lucipinnis was formally described by Wright & Bailey in 2006 and is the species almost universally sold as “Petricola Catfish” or “Synodontis petricola” in the aquarium trade. The true S. petricola is extremely rare in commerce. S. lucipinnis is smaller (to ~9 cm) and arguably better suited to home aquaria than the larger S. petricola (to ~14 cm). Both species share the same Lake Tanganyika rocky biotope origin and essentially identical care requirements.

Water chemistry is the critical constraint: Lake Tanganyika is highly alkaline and hard (pH 8.5–9.0, GH 10–20, KH 10–18), and S. lucipinnis will not thrive in the neutral or soft water tolerated by most catfish. Decor should replicate the rocky shoreline with stacked flat rocks, slate overhangs, and interconnecting caves. A group of at least 3 fish (6+ is often recommended) will display more natural social behaviour and reduced inter-specimen tension.

As a scaleless catfish, S. lucipinnis is highly sensitive to copper-based medications (treat the whole tank volume, not just an isolation tank, and avoid proprietary copper treatments entirely). Maintain excellent oxygenation: Lake Tanganyika is deep, oligotrophic, and well-oxygenated. Feeding should be timed to after lights-out given the nocturnal feeding preference; sinking pellets and wafers prevent food being intercepted by cichlid tankmates. This species is a confirmed egg-scatterer and has been bred in captivity in larger, well-established tanks.
breedingNotes: Breeding has been achieved in captivity, though not routinely. Egg-scattering spawners; eggs are deposited among rock crevices. No parental care is provided and adults will consume eggs if not removed. A single large cave or breeding tile is sometimes used as a spawning site. Raising fry requires a separate rearing tank with infusoria or finely powdered fry food followed by micro-pellets. Maturity is reached at approximately 18–24 months. A ratio of two females per male reduces conspecific stress.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Synodontis lucipinnis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 9 cm), temperature 23–27°C, pH 8.0–9.0, hardness 10–20°H, native range (Lake Tanganyika), habitat (rocky littoral), diet, compatibility, and breeding notes. Confirmed species identity issue: virtually all trade ‘S. petricola’ are S. lucipinnis.

additional.name: FishBase — Synodontis lucipinnis
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size, taxonomy (Mochokidae, Siluriformes), native range (Lake Tanganyika basin: DRC, Tanzania, Zambia), original description year (Wright & Bailey 2006). Confirmed temperature range and pH tolerance.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Synodontis Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General Synodontis care overview; confirmed omnivorous diet, sinking-food feeding strategy, group-keeping recommendation, and copper sensitivity for scaleless catfish. Supplemented hobbyist guidance on tank size and tankmate selection.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Synodontis lucipinnis species profile
additional.notes: Confirmed species-splitting history (described 2006; previously sold as S. petricola), adult size ~9 cm vs S. petricola ~14 cm, Tanganyikan hard-alkaline water requirements, nocturnal feeding, and group-keeping of 3+ individuals.

additional.name: Wright, J.J. & Bailey, R.M. (2006) — Systematic revision of the formerly monotypic genus Synodontis (Mochokidae) from Lake Tanganyika, via FishBase reference
additional.notes: Original taxonomic description reference; confirms formal description of S. lucipinnis in 2006, separating it from S. petricola based on morphometric and meristic characters including smaller adult size and fin-ray counts.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scattering spawner; breeding achieved in captivity but not routine. Eggs deposited in rock crevices; adults consume eggs if not removed. Fry require separate rearing with micro-foods. Maturity at ~18–24 months.
